# R and AI Resources

A curated list of resources for learning and using R, and for
working with AI as part of your data-science practice. Nothing here
is required reading — but each item below has earned its place.
Skim it now, bookmark it, and return as you go.

## Resources for learning and using R

### IDEs for R

[RStudio](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/) remains the
most widely used IDE for R, and, like R, it is open-source. Its
"Help" tab is a gateway to a wide range of R and RStudio
resources. However, Posit (the company behind RStudio) has released
[Positron](https://positron.posit.co/), a next-generation IDE built
for data science with both R and Python. Positron combines the
familiar feel of RStudio with the extensibility of VS Code, and
includes [built-in AI assistance](https://positron.posit.co/assistant)
via Positron Assistant. If you're comfortable with RStudio, stick
with it; if you're curious about a more modern interface or plan to
work across R and Python, Positron is worth exploring.

### Learning R

Norm Matloff, a computer scientist and statistician at UC-Davis,
has a nice,
[fast introduction to (base) R](https://github.com/matloff/fasteR?tab=readme-ov-file#overview).
He also has interesting takes on
[base R vs the Tidyverse](https://matloff.github.io/TidyverseSkeptic/)
and the
[R vs Python debate](https://github.com/matloff/R-vs.-Python-for-Data-Science)
in data science. For a more in-depth treatment, consult the widely
used [R for Data Science](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by
[Wickham](https://hadley.nz/) and
[Grolemund](https://education.rstudio.com/author/garrett/). They
will guide you through the [Tidyverse](https://www.tidyverse.org/).
[Tidy Data Tutor](https://tidydatatutor.com/) helps you visualize
your data analysis pipelines. James Scott has a
[great introduction to R](https://bookdown.org/jgscott/DSGI/) that
more clearly maps onto BUSN 5000.

You will find a list of
[econometrics packages](https://cran.r-project.org/web/views/Econometrics.html)
for R at the
[Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN)](https://cran.r-project.org/web/views/Econometrics.html).
You might prefer the
[causal inference task view](https://cran.r-project.org/web/views/CausalInference.html)
or
[machine learning task view](https://cran.r-project.org/web/views/MachineLearning.html).

Learning [R Markdown](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/) or
[Quarto](https://quarto.org/) should go hand-in-hand with learning
R. For R Markdown, get started
[here](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/lesson-1.html) and keep a
[cheatsheet](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/lesson-15.html) handy.
You can get started with Quarto
[here](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/).

### Workflow

At least as important as learning R is understanding basic workflow
principles. R-bloggers has a
[beginner's guide](https://www.r-bloggers.com/2020/01/rstudio-projects-and-working-directories-a-beginners-guide/)
using RStudio Projects. You can find some of the same principles in
[chapter 2](https://socviz.co/gettingstarted.html) of
[Kieran Healy's](https://kieranhealy.org/) Data Visualization
[book](https://socviz.co/index.html#preface) (also linked on the
course schedule).
[Matt Gentzkow](https://www.matthewgentzkow.com/) and
[Jesse Shapiro](https://shapiro.scholars.harvard.edu/) provide a
[fuller take](https://web.stanford.edu/~gentzkow/research/CodeAndData.pdf)
on workflow principles from the perspective of academic social
scientists (also posted on eLC). They even provide a
[manual](https://github.com/gentzkow/ra-manual/wiki) for doing as
they do.

For a perspective specifically on coding practices in data science
education, Pruim, Gîrjău, and Horton (2023) make the case in
["Fostering Better Coding Practices for Data Scientists"](https://hdsr.mitpress.mit.edu/pub/8wsiqh1c/release/4)
(*Harvard Data Science Review*) that good coding habits matter even
for beginners. Their argument: it's far easier to learn good
practices alongside R than to unlearn bad habits later. They
organize their guidance into a practical top-10 list. Worth reading
early in your data-science career.

Suffice it to say that Healy's book is a great introduction to the
wonders of [ggplot](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/index.html), which
is not R, but indispensable to analysis done in R.

## AI resources

### Working with AI

My take is that you should view AI chatbots, like
[ChatGPT](https://openai.com/chatgpt/overview),
[Claude](https://claude.ai/), and
[Gemini](https://gemini.google.com/app), as exceedingly productive
collaborators with whom you should learn to work, much like you
would with a human. Unfortunately, there are no secret prompting
strategies or special step-by-step manuals (also like with a
human). For some practical guidance along these lines, I recommend
that you follow
[Ethan Mollick](https://mgmt.wharton.upenn.edu/profile/emollick/)'s
[Substack](https://www.oneusefulthing.org/). If you want to swim in
the deeper end of the pool, check out the Substack,
[Don't Worry About the Vase](https://thezvi.substack.com/).

### AI for coding

One place gen AI really helps is in coding. You can get coding help
from chat interfaces, but you may prefer a more integrated
experience. Several options exist:

- [Positron Assistant](https://positron.posit.co/assistant): If you
  use Positron, this built-in AI assistant understands your R
  environment, loaded data, and console history. It integrates
  GitHub Copilot for code completions and Claude for chat and agent
  mode.
- [GitHub Copilot](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot): Works in
  RStudio, VS Code, and other IDEs.
  [Here](https://docs.github.com/en/education/explore-the-benefits-of-teaching-and-learning-with-github-education/github-education-for-students/apply-to-github-education-as-a-student)
  is how to get free access as a student.
  [Here](https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/ide/guide/tools/copilot.html)
  are instructions for RStudio integration.
- [Claude Code](https://www.anthropic.com/claude-code): A
  command-line tool that can read and modify files in your project
  directory, run commands, and understand your codebase context.
  Simon Couch has written useful posts on
  [using Claude Code for R package development](https://www.simonpcouch.com/blog/2025-03-26-claude-code/).
- [Cursor](https://www.cursor.com/): An AI-first code editor built
  on VS Code.

Alternatively, you may find you can get by just
[vibe coding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vibe_coding) with a
chatbot — describing what you want and iterating on the results.

### Learning more about AI

To learn more about AI, including a comparison of different models,
check out
[Generative AI for Beginners](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/shows/generative-ai-for-beginners/),
a free course offered by
[Microsoft Cloud Advocates](https://developer.microsoft.com/en-us/advocates/).
[OpenAI](https://academy.openai.com/) and
[Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/learn) have their own AI
academies when you want to step it up.
